title: The $250,000 YouTube Shorts Strategy Nobody's Talking About

transcript.text: A quarter of a million dollars, all from one channel in one year from YouTube Shorts. >> For the first 3 months, the channel was doing 80 to 100 million views. >> This is Tim Danilov, and in this video, he's going to show you how this channel crushed YouTube so that you can run the same plays on your channel. There are three keys to Tim's success. The untapped niche he discovered and now dominates. The strategic business move that cut his costs by over 80% and the exact YouTube strategy that you can start using right now. This is the part of YouTube nobody shows you. The systems, the margins that turn views into real income. But let's start where he started, from nowhere. He had no audience to speak of, no faith in his English skills, and he had no interest in getting in front of camera. But everyone starts with nothing until they start. >> Actually started exactly 1 year ago, so January 27th, I posted my first ever YouTube video on our award channel. >> And this channel started like any other on YouTube. It stalled, it spluttered, it failed to gather any momentum until the seventh video. >> This was the video that absolutely blew up. This one went from zero to 25 million views in like first 7 days. So, this video got us monetized on the channel. >> 25 million views. Not bad, right? And Tim monetized his channel within the first month. And with more shorts getting similar view counts, the coin really started to roll in. Now, at this point, a typical video case study might hit you with confetti and say, "Job done. It's all about consistency, being persistent, determined, and passionate." But I'm not going to do that today. >> For the first 3 months, the channel was doing 80 to 100 million views. What I didn't tell you is it was still not profitable. I was making like 6 to 7,000 dollars a month, but I was I wasn't making any money for myself. >> Here is the truth that most case studies skip. The money was real. Seven grand coming into the channel every single [music] month. The challenge was that pretty much all of that money was immediately going back out into production. Think Mr. Beast reinvesting every single dollar he earns and more back into his [music] next video. And it's the same thing here, just on a smaller scale. The income was there, but the margins simply weren't. And it all came down to that niche we were talking about earlier. Several thousand dollars to produce around 15 videos a month. >> For three months of running it negatively, I was about to close it, basically. [music] >> So, Tim is three months in, blowing up audience-wise, and yet he's still on the verge of calling it quits. But, a year later, Tim's channel is still there and thriving. So, what changed? Well, it wasn't making better videos or building higher view counts. It was actually a financial decision. First, he bought himself some runway. He sold a slice of his channel to an investor. Now, notice how he pitches this, because it's really important. >> All I asked for was $10,000 for three months of salaries of people that I plan to hire. And he was more than happy to do that for 10% of the future company. >> That investor had two pieces of advice for Tim. >> First, you need to hire full-time, so people who will actually care about the channel, who will go all-in on what they do on your channel for you. Number two, you need to hire from high ROI markets. >> Mhm, high ROI or return on investment markets. It's the name of the game. Let's break down what that all means. It's about hiring skilled creatives in regions where the cost of living is lower, so that a dollar stretches out further. Now, that's not to say that this creative is being underpaid. They're actually being overcompensated for what they would earn locally were they to get the same job. >> We went to Armenia, we went to Kazakhstan, we went to Russia, we went to Ukraine, and we found the best talent. In most of those countries, the average salary is like 700 euros, right? So, when you offer them 1.2k a month, 1.5k a month, they're amazed. Like, they're making like twice as their average salary in the country. I would hire the same like animator from US, it's like 6K minimum. >> And here is where I want you to pay close attention. >> The real genius behind it was not the the niche or the topics or the videos themselves. It was actually the hiring strategy because the only reason why this strategy was profitable is because our expenses were so low compared to all our other competitors. Like the films, he does 3 billion views a month and he's paying $1,000 per short. Like the same same quality like 1,000 bucks. And we are now averaging like 150, 160. So we pay what what's that? Like five five times less for almost the same quality. >> So while every other creator was obsessed with views, Tim was focusing on reducing costs without sacrificing the quality of his content. And once you can do that, you let the maths take care of themselves. Kind of. >> We four-Xed the amount of content because I basically four-Xed the team. So we hired like five animators at the same time and with that we started producing 60 videos and that's that's how the whole jump happened. >> And you don't have to imagine what that does to the revenue of a channel. Because Tim showed us. And it only took 60 days. >> We basically went from $7,000 in April to $50,000 in in June in just like 60 days. >> And it's not just about the huge increase in revenue that the channel was making. Tim also pulled this off without chaining himself to the computer 24/7. >> I was planning the channel as a business not as a creative side hustle. Once they were built, I didn't spend more than 10 hours a week managing the channel. >> 10 hours a week. Have you got creator envy? Because I sure do. But let's move on to the question you actually came here for. How do you find a market or a niche that's both lucrative and wide open for you to enter? >> Your YouTube like faceless YouTube niche is the combination of the format and the market. And what the niche bending is is you create a new niche by applying the viral format to the market where it was never used before. >> then, niche bending. It's a fancy new way of explaining a tried, tested, and successful concept. As a very quick example, take the channel TierZoo. It took the formats of tier lists and video gaming culture and smashed it together with zoology education. >> In the current meta, one class has dominated the competitive scene, the mammals. Ever since the devs rolled out harsh nerfs to the Arcisaur faction, mammals have become the most successful archetype in the game. >> So this is how Tim built that first huge channel with billions of views called The Art of War. He didn't try to invent a brand new topic from scratch. Instead, he found a format that was resonating with an audience from a channel that already existed called Zac D Films and applied it to a new niche. And by doing that, he found a brand new audience. >> I just [music] filtered his most popular videos and I found that 40% of his most popular videos were about uh guns and I was like, what if I do a whole channel about guns? >> And so this is a move worth learning. First, find the biggest creators in your space and then find their biggest outliers, which by the way is really easy to do with our free ViddIQ tool. And then, you want to look for those patterns that they're sitting on but they haven't [music] fully committed to. Now of course, it is important to mention at this point that you shouldn't clone other people's content because first of all, that's essentially theft. And secondly, it very rarely works. But what you can do is build a brand new channel from all of the ideas and the formats that these creators basically left on the table. And there is one more magic ingredient to this formula. >> The simpler your niche, the quicker it gets saturated. The harder your niche to replicate, the more time you actually have to make money with it because like you'll just have this niche for yourself. You'll just mon- monopolize it before everyone else can get in. >> Tim searched out one of the hardest niches on purpose and now he completely owns it. He dominates it. Now, you might be looking at $10,000, an investor and thinking to yourself, this is a part of a strategy I can't realistically copy. But I have some good news. You don't need either one of these to start. There are two real ways here and Tim has used both. One does scale faster when you bring in a partner. The other you can start today for free. But to continue Tim's story first, he did bring in a partner. Now, that can be an investor, but it could just as easily be another creator, somebody who's just as fired up as you about a YouTube idea. Someone that you could team up with as co-founders so that you split the work, but also the upside. Either way, what made this work for Tim was the pitch. He didn't ask for a favor, he brought an opportunity >> [music] >> and a plan. >> I also knew that he wanted to expand in different social media accounts and I was like, "Can I be your case study? Can I just open you this kind of like uh door uh to YouTube and I'm going to do everything myself?" >> As for the second path, well, that's one that all of you can use right now. Start with nothing. >> [clears throat] >> AI is free. If you go into the YouTube app, you can prompt anything into existence through YouTube Shorts and it doesn't cost you a dime. But obviously, you need to create this AI content the right way, not just slop. >> There is a proper use of AI where you build a connection with the audience and the audience trusts you. So, it might be an AI influencer talking about finance. We saw channels like, for example, Faster Fred do really well. >> And here's another AI stack that you could potentially use. Nano banana for the images, Grock or Cling to animate, CapCut to assemble and add the subtitles. The barrier in this day and age is not money. It is lower than what you think, and that is exactly why it works. But, it is now time for the honest disclaimer. I don't want to sell you the fantasy that this is the perfect solution for big revenue on YouTube Shorts. It is still a very volatile part of the platform. Shorts can turn on you fast. >> Around November, basically the whole Shorts algorithm was flooded. 33% of the feed is now AI slop. Those 3D videos, I think they got messed up with the AI slop. YouTube just started to deprioritize the the 3D videos. >> And at this point, Tim does what every good creator is willing to do, not turn themselves into the victim. Instead, he reads the market and adapts when it begins to shift. >> It's been 19 days and we haven't posted a single video. It's not always the the wins. Sometimes you go through, you know, tough times, but you have to keep going. >> In this case, the market cooled off for one of his formats, so he's reallocated, easing off on that channel and putting his team's energy where the momentum is right now. But, notice how he can survive bad months that completely wipe out his competitors. It is the same reason he was successful in the first place. His costs are so low that the channel remains profitable even when views are falling off a cliff. If we strip away Tim's story, what we're left with is the strategy. Five steps in order. You're welcome to screenshot this. Find an underserved niche by filtering a bigger creator's best videos for the patterns they're not fully chasing. Take a proven format and bend it into a niche or a market that's never seen it before. Hire full-time from low-cost, high-talented regions, so your cost per video is a fraction of your competitors. Then, once the channel is proven to work, scale your team and your video output. And always keep an eye on those costs to make sure they're low enough that you stay profitable even through the bad months. And notice what's not on the list, going viral or being in front of camera. This is about understanding YouTube and treating it as a business. Today, a little over a year since Tim started, he doesn't just run one channel anymore. He has a portfolio of channels, a real company behind them. >> What I want to get to is 10 branded faceless channels in a portfolio, doing about $2 million a year by the end of this year. Across all of the all of our projects, we now have 44 people working for us full-time. >> All of which leaves us with one burning question. Why gift all of these strategies and all of these insights to people who could well become Tim's competition by this time next week? >> I'm here to help creators heal from this disease, which is basically doing everything by themselves and thinking they will still somehow grow because they won't. >> So, I want to hear from you, whether you are already running a faceless channel or you're thinking of starting one. Has all of this inspired you? And which move are you going to try first? Let us know in the comments below. Now, in this video, we have briefly mentioned the niche bending strategy. But to fully implement it, you're going to need more examples and actionable steps on exactly what to do, which is why I've made this complete guide over here, which is available right now. I'll see you in there.
